text: The patient is positioned in a flank position for the nephrectomy procedure. The tumor is located on the right side, with visible ribs and costal margin. Due to the patient's severe scoliosis, the surgical space is notably reduced.

The surgeon marks the costal margin, iliac crest, and pararectal line on the patient's flank for trocar placement. A varus needle is inserted, and pneumoperitoneum is insufflated to a pressure of ten, preparing for kidney surgery.

The surgeon marks the trochanter and positions the trocars for the nephrectomy, using a ten millimeter camera for enhanced vision quality. The surgical approach involves placing the trocars two finger breadths below the costal margin on the right side, considering the proximity of the liver.

The surgeon prepares to make an incision for the ten millimeter camera port, positioning the optical trocar and working ports in parallel rather than the typical triangular configuration. The varus needle is used to ensure visibility and avoid bowel injury during entry.

The surgeon uses a visual trocar with the camera inserted to navigate through the different fascia layers. The endoscopic view reveals layers of fat, muscle fascia, muscle, and peritoneum as the trocar progresses inward.

The surgeon advances the instrument through the layers of fascia, identifying whitish tissue as fascia before reaching the muscle layer. The liver edge is visible, indicating a safe entry into the peritoneum. The surgeon checks for any injury to the bowel or vessels upon entry.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree lens to visualize the surgical field, marking the rectus muscle and identifying the epigastric vessels. The trochanter is positioned at the junction between the rectus muscle and the lateral muscle to avoid injuring the epigastric vessels.

The surgeon uses a twelve millimeter trocar for hemolog placement, ensuring it is positioned safely away from the epigastric vessels. The trocar is inserted, and the gas is switched, avoiding placement at the trocar entry site.

The surgeon positions large trocars for optimal vision during the nephrectomy procedure. A lens and tweezers are prepared for use, and the surgeon prefers using bipolar-monopolar instruments for dissection, along with sealers for tissue sealing.

The surgeon uses an Ethicon end seal, a bipolar instrument, to dissect the triangular ligament of the liver, ensuring the liver is well separated for the dissection of a visible hilar tumor. The triangular ligament is dissected up to the crux of the diaphragm, with a self-retractor placed to maintain exposure. The phrenic vessel is visible during this process.

The surgeon aggressively mobilizes the angle of the liver, freeing it up to facilitate retraction and elevation. The liver is slightly attached, but the surgeon plans to release it, removing any surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon uses a bipolar sealant to carefully dissect near the diaphragm, ensuring that the instrument does not penetrate the pleura. Additional tissue is freed to allow proper anchoring of the dissector.

The surgeon spends several minutes freeing the triangular ligament of the liver to achieve a clear exposure of the upper pole, which is crucial for visualizing the kidney. Using the left hand, the surgeon reflects the liver, revealing the phrenic vessel and approaching the crux of the diaphragm. A 5mm trocar is placed at the level of the xiphoid process.
The surgeon places an accessory five millimeter trocar at the level of the xiphoid and attempts to maneuver under the liver, encountering a small amount of fat tissue.
The surgeon is positioned under the liver, having freed the triangular ligament to facilitate upward movement. The duodenum is visible, along with the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the tumor.
The surgeon begins dissecting the colon to identify and isolate the ureter, which is visually important due to the tumor's proximity to the ureter.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field, with the inferior vena cava (IVC) clearly identifiable nearby.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland and the renal vein, noting a branch of the renal vein with a double vein structure. The surgical team works to expose this area further and attempts to elevate the liver slightly.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein and notes the proximity of the ureter. The dissection plane reveals yellow mesenteric fat and translucent retroperitoneal fat.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and gonadal vein, ensuring a clear plane of dissection. A trocar is switched to an asson type, which remains anchored to the skin to prevent movement during the procedure.
The surgeon points out a double vein and the inferior vena cava (IVC) in the surgical field, with anticipation of identifying the artery nearby.

The surgeon identifies the renal artery, which appears small and likely has a branch nearby. The surgeon begins dissection around the artery, using a five millimeter instrument through a larger trocar, which causes visible tremor due to the size mismatch.

The ureter is clearly visible in the surgical field. The surgeon begins to free the ureter, discussing the potential use of a fourth trocar and a third assistant to elevate the kidney for better visualization. Dr. Romain Boissier from the University of Marseille is mentioned as preparing to assist in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the main renal artery branch, preparing to clamp it for better visibility during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the common branch of the artery, which bifurcates, appearing thin with no other arteries visible behind it. The tumor is visible, and the artery has been dissected.

The surgeon identifies the tumor positioned adjacent to the urethra and vein. The plan is to de-fat the tumor while preserving the fat on top for anchoring purposes. The urethra is carefully dissected to ensure clear separation from the tumor.

The surgeon identifies a vein adjacent to the tumor and considers lifting the tumor to enucleate it over the vein. The vein is clearly visible, and the surgeon contemplates clamping it to prevent bleeding during the dissection.

The surgeon visually identifies the demarcation of the tumor, emphasizing the necessity of enucleation due to the proximity of vessels and the urethra. The tumor is being defatted, with anatomical structures being reconstructed in a three-dimensional view.

The surgeon uses a lateral trocar and an Enseal device to work on the surgical field, focusing on an artery visible in the area. The assistant is asked to come closer to provide better visualization and support during the procedure.

The surgeon discusses the strategy to start enucleation on the left side where the tumor is free, aiming to push the tumor upwards to create space between the vein, artery, urethra, and tumor. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of gaining space in this area before proceeding with enucleation.

The surgeon uses the Enseal device for gentle dissection, revealing branches of the artery and vein that appear to be feeding the tumor. The branches are visually identified, but the decision to clamp or cut them is still under consideration due to their potential connection to the ureter and other parts of the kidney.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the artery, aiming to skeletonize it and identify branches that supply the lower pole of the kidney. The goal is to preserve these branches during enucleation to minimize damage to healthy kidney parenchyma.
The surgeon marks the dissection margin on the kidney, pushing the tumor towards themselves to delineate the boundary for enucleation. The tumor's movement is visible as the margin is outlined.

The surgeon marks the area for dissection, adjusting the position to ensure precise delineation. Using tweezers, the surgeon prepares to clean the surgical field for better visibility before proceeding with the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and renal pelvis, noting their proximity to the tumor. The camera view shifts to reveal an artery branch, which is traced as it extends over and behind the tumor area.

The surgeon carefully dissects the veins, considering options for clamping. The camera moves closer to provide a detailed view of the veins, with the surgeon contemplating whether to clamp certain veins while leaving others open.
The surgeon prepares to clamp the artery using a Bulldog clamp, positioning it carefully. The Bulldog and Scanlan clamps are visible, tools commonly used in robotic surgery.
The surgeon uses scissors to enter the surgical field, revealing the tumor. The approach involves addressing the vessels first before proceeding towards them.

The surgeon navigates around the tumor, clearly visualizing its capsule. Using scissors with the tip down, they avoid penetrating the tumor. A small vessel is identified and bleeding is observed, indicating it is a vein. The surgeon decides to proceed without addressing the vessel immediately.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the scissors around the visible tumor, adjusting the tip direction to avoid penetrating the tumor capsule. The tumor is observed turning, prompting a change in scissor orientation. The surgeon identifies a vessel near the tumor, noting its proximity and potential risk, while highlighting the vein's location.
The surgeon identifies the artery and prepares to apply a Hem-o-lok clip to secure it, ensuring careful placement without urgency.

The surgeon examines the surgical field, identifying a vein that is not bleeding significantly. The decision is made to leave the vein unclamped for now, with plans to potentially clip or suture it after completing the dissection.

The surgeon navigates around the tumor capsule, avoiding entry into the parenchyma. A vessel is identified as entering the tumor, indicating it cannot be preserved. The surgical field is adjusted for better visualization.

The surgeon identifies a structure that may be a calyx or vein near the tumor, exercising caution due to proximity to the tumor. Suction is applied to manage minor bleeding, and the surgeon clamps the vein for better visibility.

The surgeon uses a bulldog clamp to attempt clamping a major branch of a vein near the tumor. The tumor is visibly very close to the clamped area, but the surgeon avoids entering the tumor. The vein is identified adjacent to the tumor.

The surgeon clamps the outer branch of a vein using a bulldog clamp, assessing whether this action stops the bleeding. The clip is placed on a calyx, and the bleeding ceases, indicating the vein was the source. The surgical field shows the other side of the dissection, with the next step being closure of the area.
The surgeon carefully lifts the tumor, ensuring minimal cutting of the surrounding renal parenchyma to maintain a clear margin.

The surgeon performs a partial nephrectomy, lifting the tumor from the kidney and inspecting the margins to ensure complete removal without cutting into the tumor. The surgical field shows clear margins indicating successful enucleation.

The surgeon uses suction to clear the field and prepares to clamp the vein, emphasizing the importance of suturing it properly to prevent bleeding. The surgical field shows no parenchyma, and the vein is visibly open, requiring careful suturing. The camera is positioned between the surgeon's arms for optimal triangulation.

The surgeon places a double hemlock to secure the suture, opting for separated sutures to manage tension. The tissue is gradually approximated, resembling the closing of a book, with plans to tie everything at the end.
The surgeon places another suture in the renal parenchyma, aiming to approximate the tissue edges for closure.
The surgeon approximates tissue to compress any open veins, using flow seal and surgicel for additional compression. The surgeon ties and pulls on a suture to further approximate the tissue.

The surgeon pulls on the suture to approximate tissue, using monocryl for closure. A hemlock clip is applied to secure the suture. The surgical field shows the effects of warm ischemia, with visible tissue changes after 21 minutes.

The surgeon de-clamps the renal vein followed by the renal artery, observing the immediate changes in blood flow and tissue perfusion. The surgical field is cleared of clamps, and the team notes the elapsed time of warm ischemia.
The surgeon observes the kidney, noting its healthy color and absence of bleeding, while utilizing robotic surgery for efficiency.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut sutures and places the excised tumor into a specimen bag. The surgeon then retroperitonealizes the area to apply compression on top of the kidney, noting the preservation of a small artery.
